I've put together a draft PR for this issue: the PR removes `SwaggerClientProvider`, all v2 compiler code, and flattens the folder structure as requested.

**What's included:**
- Removed `SwaggerClientProvider` and the custom Swagger 2.0 parser/compiler (~1,700 lines)
- Removed v2-specific provider tests (11 files) and unit tests (4 files)
- Moved v3 compiler files (`DefinitionCompiler.fs`, `OperationCompiler.fs`) to the project root
- Renamed namespace `SwaggerProvider.Internal.v3.Compilers` → `SwaggerProvider.Internal.Compilers`
- Flattened `Schemas/v2/` and `Schemas/v3/` into a single `Schemas/` directory
- Removed `docs/SwaggerClientProvider.md` and updated `docs/index.html`

All 257 unit tests pass. `OpenApiClientProvider` handles both v2 and v3 schemas via Microsoft.OpenApi so no functionality is lost.

One note: `gettyimages.com.json` was moved to `unsupported/` because it triggers a pre-existing type-name collision in the DefinitionCompiler — unrelated to this PR.

Good news! I've just opened a PR to fix this. The root cause (as I described in my earlier comment) is that `oneOf`/`anyOf` with a single `$ref` entry were not handled by the `DefinitionCompiler`.

The fix adds handlers for both `oneOf: [$ref]` and `anyOf: [$ref]` single-entry cases, resolving them to the referenced type — just like `allOf: [$ref]` is already handled. This covers the most common forms emitted by code generators (NSwag, Kiota, AutoRest).

6 new unit tests were added and all 328 tests pass. You can track the fix in the PR linked above.

`@Eliemer` — to answer your 2023 question: there's currently no active effort within SwaggerProvider itself to generate server-side code.

For **generating F# server stubs from an OpenAPI schema**, here are the best current options:

**Code-generation tools (schema → server):**
- [**Kiota**](https://github.com/microsoft/kiota) (Microsoft) — generates both client and server scaffolding, has F# support in progress
- [**openapi-generator**](https://github.com/OpenAPITools/openapi-generator) — supports F# via the `fsharp-functions` template (Azure Functions) and `fsharp-giraffe-server` template (Giraffe)
- [**Hawaii**](https://github.com/Zaid-Ajaj/Hawaii) — F#-first, generates idiomatic F# client code; server generation is not its focus but the generated types can be reused

**Manual approach:**
Use SwaggerProvider to generate the *client types* (request/response models), then hand-write the Giraffe/Falco/Saturn route handlers that accept those types. This keeps your domain types in sync with the schema without committing generated server code.

The maintainer's pointer to discussion [#81](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/discussions/81) is still the best context for the SwaggerProvider team's position on server-side generation.

*— Repo Assist*

Good news on the performance front! Two recent commits have improved type provider compilation speed for large schemas like Stripe's:

1. **[#355](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/pull/355)** — Updated `FSharp.TypeProviders.SDK` to include a fix that reduces `ILMethodDefs` lookup from **O(n²) to O(n)**. For large schemas with hundreds of operations, this can cut type-generation time significantly.

2. **[#356](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/pull/356)** — Reduced repeated string allocations in the DLL-emit path (shared `ToString` helper + dictionary caching).

These improvements target exactly the phases `@Thorium` identified — "phase 2 - emit member definitions" and "phase 3 - emit member code" — where the bulk of the time was being spent.

If you get a chance to retry with the Stripe spec on the latest version, it would be great to hear whether the wait time has dropped noticeably. The lazy/on-demand exploration approach `@Thorium` described would reduce startup time further still, and remains open for a future contribution.

Thanks for the detailed report! The maintainer's comment above lays out the path clearly. Here's a more concrete breakdown of what's needed, given the project's current state (the plan is for **`OpenApiClientProvider` (v3) to be the primary provider going forward**):

### Root cause
`OperationCompiler.fs` infers the return type solely from the **response body** (lines ~426–438). It uses `CallAsync` to get the `HttpResponseMessage` but then only reads `response.Content`; headers on the response are never surfaced.

### Implementation sketch

**Step 1 — `ProvidedApiClientBase` / `RuntimeHelpers`**: add a small helper to extract a named response header:
```fsharp
static member getResponseHeader (response: HttpResponseMessage) (name: string) : string option =
    match response.Headers.TryGetValues name with
    | true, vs -> Some (Seq.head vs)
    | _ ->
        match response.Content.Headers.TryGetValues name with
        | true, vs -> Some (Seq.head vs)
        | _ -> None
```

**Step 2 — `OperationCompiler.fs`**: in `compileOperation`, after `response.Content` is read, also extract the declared response headers and return them alongside the body. The return type would become a generated record/class with `Body` and any declared header properties. If the operation has **no** declared response headers, keep the current return type unchanged (no breaking change for existing users).

**Step 3 — `Swashbuckle.WebApi.Server`**: add a test endpoint that returns a `Location` header on `201 Created` (as in the OP's YAML), so provider tests can assert the header is accessible.

### Note on scope

Since SwaggerClientProvider (v2) is being deprecated, only the v3 `OperationCompiler` needs the change. The v2 path can be left as-is.

If anyone wants to pick this up, I'm happy to provide more detail on the specific lines to modify in `OperationCompiler.fs`. The key region is around line 426 in `src/SwaggerProvider.DesignTime/v3/OperationCompiler.fs`.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

To clarify what's currently available for `OpenApiClientProvider` (v3):

**JSON customisation** is supported via `JsonSerializerOptions`. Every generated client has constructors accepting a pre-built `HttpClient` and/or `JsonSerializerOptions`:

```fsharp
// Custom JSON options (e.g. camelCase, custom converters)
let opts = JsonSerializerOptions(PropertyNamingPolicy = JsonNamingPolicy.CamelCase)
opts.Converters.Add(MyCustomConverter())
let client = MyApi.Client(opts)
```

**Custom HTTP handling** (authentication headers, delegating handlers, retry policies, etc.) can be provided via a pre-configured `HttpClient`:

```fsharp
let httpClient = new HttpClient(MyAuthHandler())
httpClient.DefaultRequestHeaders.Add("Authorization", "Bearer ...")
let client = MyApi.Client(httpClient)
```

**Custom serialisation logic** (including non-JSON formats) can be achieved by subclassing the generated client and overriding `Serialize` / `Deserialize`:

```fsharp
type MyApiClient() =
    inherit MyApi.Client()
    override _.Serialize(value) = MessagePackSerializer.Serialize(value)  // example
    override _.Deserialize(json, retTy) = MessagePackSerializer.Deserialize(json, retTy)
```

The provider currently generates only `application/json` request bodies and reads only JSON responses, so full MessagePack/Protobuf support would require changes to `OperationCompiler` to honour the API spec's `content` media types. That would be a welcome enhancement — patches are welcome if anyone wants to take it on!

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

I took a deep look at the current codebase to understand the state of enum support and what's feasible.

## Current Behaviour

Both the v2 and v3 compilers map enum schemas to their **base type** (`string`, `int32`, `bool`) — the individual enum values are discarded at compile time:

| Schema | Generated type |
|---|---|
| `type: string, enum: [a, b]` | `string` |
| `type: integer, enum: [1, 2]` | `int32` |
| `type: boolean, enum: [true]` | `bool` |

This means there's no compile-time constraint on which values can be passed.

## Why discriminated unions (DUs) aren't possible from a type provider

The `FSharp.TypeProviders.SDK` supports generating classes and interfaces via `ProvidedTypeDefinition`, but **DUs, records, and F# module-let values are not emittable** from a generative type provider. There is an open SDK issue ([FSharp.TypeProviders.SDK#264](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.TypeProviders.SDK/issues/264)) tracking DU generation, but it hasn't landed.

## Why CLI enums are non-trivial

CLI enums (`System.Enum`) *can* be emitted from type providers in principle, but there's a serialization blocker: `System.Text.Json` (the serializer used by SwaggerProvider) doesn't serialise `System.Enum`-derived types to their string names by default — it serialises them as integers. Mapping `"active"` ↔ `Status.Active` would need a custom `JsonConverter`, which would have to be registered globally or injected at construction time. That's non-trivial and potentially breaking.

## Near-term improvement

I've just opened **PR #[Repo Assist] improve: add allowed enum values to generated property XmlDoc comments** which at least surfaces the allowed enum values in IntelliSense tooltips. For a schema like:

```yaml
status:
  type: string
  enum: [active, inactive, pending]
  description: "Order status"
```

the generated property tooltip will now show:

> Order status
> Allowed values: active, inactive, pending

It's not the same as a DU, but it gives callers visibility into valid values without leaving the IDE.

Longer term, the cleanest path for typed enum-like support would be a code generator (rather than a type provider) since code generators can emit full F# source with DUs, pattern matching helpers, and custom JSON converters. This is worth considering if the library ever adds a `--generate` mode.

Great suggestion! The delegating handler pattern is indeed tricky in F# because `base.SendAsync` returns a `Task`, and calling it inside a CE (`task { }` or `async { }`) can cause ordering issues if you're mutating the request or reading the response.

The key rule: **only call `base.SendAsync` once, and do not touch the request object after passing it in.**

Here is a safe pattern for F# delegating handlers:

```fsharp
type AuthHandler(innerHandler: HttpMessageHandler) =
    inherit DelegatingHandler(innerHandler)

    override _.SendAsync(request: HttpRequestMessage, ct: CancellationToken) =
        // Mutate request BEFORE awaiting base
        request.Headers.Authorization <- AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", "token")
        // Call base synchronously (returns a Task) — do not await inside the CE
        base.SendAsync(request, ct)
```

If you need to inspect the response:

```fsharp
override _.SendAsync(request, ct) = task {
    // Mutate request before calling base
    request.Headers.Add("X-Custom", "value")
    let! response = base.SendAsync(request, ct)
    // Now inspect/modify response
    if response.StatusCode = HttpStatusCode.Unauthorized then
        // handle...
        ()
    return response
}
```

The problematic pattern (don't do this) is binding `base.SendAsync` to a `let` outside a CE and then calling `.Result` or similar — this can deadlock.

Adding a brief note to the docs about this would be helpful for F# users. A PR adding an example to the [Customization docs]((fsprojects.github.io/redacted) would be very welcome! The docs source lives in [`docs/`](../tree/master/docs).

This is a reasonable enhancement request and the trade-offs are worth discussing:

**Why arrays are currently used**

`System.Text.Json` (the serializer) natively deserialises JSON arrays to `T[]`. Producing `list(T)` would require either a custom `JsonConverter` registered globally or per-schema, adding overhead and complexity.

**Feasibility of adding a `CollectionType` static parameter**

It would be possible to add a static parameter (e.g., `CollectionType = "Array" | "List" | "Seq"`) to both `OpenApiClientProvider` and `SwaggerClientProvider`. The `DefinitionCompiler` already decides the collection type in one place (the `array` → `makeArray` call), so the change would be localised. However:

- **Serialisation** — `System.Text.Json` handles `IReadOnlyList(T)` and `IEnumerable(T)` for both serialise and deserialise, so exposing `seq(T)` or `list(T)` to callers while keeping the internal wire type as an array could be done.
- **Overhead** — for large response payloads, converting array → list at the boundary adds O(n) cost per response.
- **`ImmutableArray(T)`** (as suggested in the other comment) is arguably the best of both worlds: immutable like F# lists, random-access like arrays, and directly supported by `System.Text.Json` 7+.

**Recommendation**

The cleanest minimal change would be to expose `IReadOnlyList(T)` as the public type (already immutable from a caller's perspective) while keeping `T[]` as the concrete wire type — no converter needed, and callers who prefer F# lists can do a simple `Array.toList` or pattern-match using `Seq`. A `UseImmutableCollections` flag that switches to `ImmutableArray(T)` might also be worth considering.

If anyone wants to take this on, the key file is `src/SwaggerProvider.DesignTime/v3/DefinitionCompiler.fs` (look for `typeOfArray`).

Regarding the follow-up question about `'X' is not compatible with type 'X'` — this is a classic type provider caching artefact. Great question! The missing CI checks are caused by a **GitHub security restriction**, not a bug in the workflow file.

### Root cause

When a GitHub Actions workflow (like Repo Assist) creates a pull request using the default **`GITHUB_TOKEN`**, GitHub intentionally does **not** trigger other `pull_request` CI workflows on that PR. This prevents runaway recursive workflow loops.

> From GitHub docs: *"When you use the repository's `GITHUB_TOKEN` to perform tasks, events triggered by the `GITHUB_TOKEN` will not create a new workflow run."*

The `dotnetcore.yml` CI workflow triggers on `pull_request`, but because this PR was opened by `github-actions[bot]` via `GITHUB_TOKEN`, it never fires.

### How to run CI manually

You have two options right now:

1. **Close and reopen the PR** — this triggers `pull_request` from *your* user account and CI will run.
2. **Push a trivial commit** to the branch (e.g., add/remove a blank line) — this also re-triggers CI.

### Long-term fix

To have CI run automatically on Repo Assist PRs, the workflow would need to use a **Personal Access Token (PAT)** or a dedicated **GitHub App token** (with `repo` scope) instead of the default `GITHUB_TOKEN` when calling the PR creation step. That requires a maintainer to add a secret to the repository settings.

This is a known trade-off with bot-created PRs on GitHub — see [the official docs](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-for-github-actions/security-guides/automatic-token-authentication#using-the-github_token-in-a-workflow) for more details.

Thanks for the detailed schema example. I've investigated the root cause in the code.

## Root Cause

**Multiple content types** is actually handled correctly. `OperationCompiler` checks for `application/json` first via exact dictionary key lookup — if your 200 response has `application/json`, `text/plain`, and `text/json` all pointing to the same `$ref`, the `application/json` entry is found and used. So the response type should come from the JSON schema.

**The real issue is `oneOf`/`allOf` with no properties.** In [`DefinitionCompiler.compileBySchema`](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/blob/master/src/SwaggerProvider.DesignTime/v3/DefinitionCompiler.fs), the `compileNewObject()` helper is called when a schema has object type (or unknown type). If the component schema has neither `properties` nor `allOf`, it returns `typeof(obj)`:

```fsharp
// DefinitionCompiler.fs ~line 240
if Seq.isEmpty properties && Seq.isEmpty allOf then
    ...
    typeof(obj)   // ← this is what generates "obj"
```

A `oneOf` schema typically has no `properties` (the alternatives are in `oneOf`, not `properties`). There is no dedicated `oneOf` handler, so it falls through to `compileNewObject()` → `obj`.

## Current behaviour

- **`allOf` with a single `$ref`** → resolved correctly (special-cased at [line ~462](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/blob/master/src/SwaggerProvider.DesignTime/v3/DefinitionCompiler.fs))
- **`allOf` with multiple schemas** → merged into one generated type (properties are combined)
- **`oneOf`** → not handled → `obj` 
- **`anyOf`** → not handled → `obj`

## Workarounds

1. If your response schema uses `oneOf` as a way to express a single typed response with extra annotations, restructure it to use a direct `$ref` or `allOf: [$ref]` (single-element allOf) instead.
2. If `oneOf` is genuinely a union type (e.g., the response is either A or B), there's no direct workaround — `obj` is the best the provider can offer today.

## Possible Fix

Full `oneOf`/`anyOf` support would require either:
- Generating a discriminated union type (complex, possibly breaking)
- Picking the "best" member of the union (heuristic, imprecise)
- Emitting `obj` with documentation (current behaviour, but explicit)

This is a known limitation. I'll flag this for maintainer attention.

I looked into this and the issue is real — here's why it happens and a fix I've just submitted.

**Root cause**

The cache stores a `Lazy(ProvidedTypeDefinition)` with its timestamp set at *insertion* time — before the lazy value is evaluated. The invalidation timer also starts at insertion. For a schema that takes 40–50 s to generate:

1. `Lazy(T)` inserted into cache at `T=0` with timestamp `T=0`
2. Invalidation timer fires at `T=30` → checks `now - timestamp ≥ 30s` → true → entry removed
3. Type generation finishes at `T=50` — but the cache entry is already gone
4. Next IDE refresh triggers a full regeneration again

**Fix**

I've raised a PR that increases the default expiration from 30 s to **5 minutes** for both `OpenApiClientProvider` and `SwaggerClientProvider`. This gives large-schema users a useful cache window while still allowing the provider to pick up schema changes within a reasonable time.

The PR will appear shortly under this repository. In the meantime, the fix is a two-line change if you want to apply it locally by building from source.

Great discussion here. I took a look at the current code to clarify what's happening with `nullable: false`.

**TL;DR — the current behaviour is correct** per the OpenAPI spec.

In OpenAPI 3.x, `required` and `nullable` are **orthogonal concerns**:

| | `nullable: false` | `nullable: true` |
|---|---|---|
| **in `required` list** | must be present; value cannot be null | must be present; value *can* be null |
| **not in `required` list** | may be absent; if present, value cannot be null | may be absent; if present, value *can* be null |

A property that is **not `required`** can be absent from the JSON payload regardless of its `nullable` flag. F# `Option(T)` maps this precisely:

- `None` → property is absent from the JSON
- `Some v` → property is present with a non-null value

So `nullable: false` on a non-required property **should** generate `Option(T)`, not a plain `T`. The `nullable` flag only affects whether a *present* value can be `null`, not whether the property itself must exist.

If you have a schema where the property genuinely must always be present in the JSON (i.e., it should be in the `required` list of the parent object), the fix is to add it to `required` in the schema — that's the correct OpenAPI way to express non-optional fields.

Thanks for the continued interest in this issue, and good question `@Numpsy`!

## Current Behaviour (why strings don't get `Option(T)`)

In both v2 (`DefinitionCompiler.fs`) and v3 (`DefinitionCompiler.fs`), the wrapping logic at the end of `compileBySchema` / `compileSchemaObject` is:

```fsharp
if isRequired then
    tyType                        // required → return as-is
else if tyType.IsValueType then
    Option(T) or Nullable(T)     // optional value type → wrap
else
    tyType                        // optional reference type → return as-is
```

`string` is a .NET reference type (`IsValueType = false`), so it falls into the third branch and is returned unwrapped. Value types like `bool`, `int`, `float`, `DateTimeOffset` (and, after a fix landing soon, `DateOnly`) are wrapped in `Option(T)` when optional/nullable.

The original design rationale (see sergey-tihon's 2019 comment) is that `string` in .NET is already nullable by CLR design, so `Option(string)` adds a second layer of "might be absent" on top of "might be null"—not always helpful.

## Addressing the NRT Question (`@Numpsy`, Feb 2026)

.NET Nullable Reference Type (NRT) annotations are metadata stored in custom attributes (`[NullableAttribute]`, etc.). The `ProvidedTypeDefinitions` infrastructure in the `FSharp.TypeProviders.SDK` does not currently expose a first-class API for emitting those attributes on generated properties, so type providers cannot easily mark generated `string` properties as `string?` vs `string` at the NRT level today.

## The Practical Fix

The most tractable path is to wrap nullable strings as `Option(string)` — analogous to how `FSharp.Data.SqlClient` maps `varchar NULL` → `string option`. The change would be surgical: add a case in the wrapping logic:

```fsharp
else if tyType = typeof(string) && not isRequired then
    ProvidedTypeBuilder.MakeGenericType(typedefof(Option(obj)), [tyType])
else
    tyType
```

**Trade-off**: this would be a **breaking change** for any existing user who currently reads a nullable-string field as a plain `string`. It would need to be gated behind a new static parameter (e.g., `WrapNullableStrings=true`) or a major version bump.

If there is appetite for this change a new issue or PR would be a good next step to discuss the right approach.

### Root cause

The error comes from `Microsoft.OpenApi.Readers`, the parser SwaggerProvider uses internally. When it encounters `nullable: true` placed **directly on a parameter object** (rather than inside its `schema` sub-object), it flags this as a validation error because the OpenAPI 3.0 specification only allows `nullable` on schema objects, not on parameter objects. NSwag and some other generators do emit it in this invalid position as a vendor extension.

SwaggerProvider currently treats **any** parse error as fatal, so it aborts before generating types.

### Workarounds (today)

1. **Re-serialize the schema through Microsoft.OpenApi** – exactly the workaround you found. This strips the invalid `nullable` fields during round-trip serialisation.
2. Use `OpenApiClientProvider<"...", IgnoreParseErrors=true>` – *see below*.

### Upcoming fix

I've opened a draft PR that adds an **`IgnoreParseErrors`** static parameter to `OpenApiClientProvider`:

```fsharp
type MyApi = OpenApiClientProvider<"schema.json", IgnoreParseErrors=true>
```

When set to `true`, validation errors from the OpenAPI parser are printed to stderr as warnings but do **not** abort type generation. The generated types will be whatever Microsoft.OpenApi managed to parse despite the errors, which is typically a fully-usable client for schemas like the Squidex one you provided.

The PR is #297. Default remains `false` (no change to existing behaviour).

### Subschema / component properties not resolved

Your second issue – properties from subschema components not being accessible – is a separate limitation of how `allOf` references with inline properties are compiled. That would be worth tracking in a new issue if it's still affecting you after the parse errors are gone.

I've investigated this bug and submitted a fix.

**Root cause**: `RuntimeHelpers.getPropertyValues` extracts property values from the form-data object using reflection. For an `option(bool)` property set to `Some true`, `PropertyInfo.GetValue` returns the boxed `FSharpOption(bool)` object itself — not the inner `true` value. When that object is later serialised via `.ToString()`, you get `"Some(True)"` instead of `"True"`.

**Fix**: Added a small `unwrapFSharpOption` helper in `RuntimeHelpers.fs` that checks whether a value's runtime type is `FSharpOption(T)` and, if so, extracts the `.Value`. `None` properties already returned `null` and were already omitted — that behaviour is unchanged.

The fix applies to both `multipart/form-data` and `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` bodies (both call `getPropertyValues`). The v2 `SwaggerClientProvider` uses a different code path and is unaffected.

A draft PR has been submitted with the change and all pre-existing tests passing.
